1: YHWH spoke to Moses — Leviticus 20 provides the capital punishment schedule for violations enumerated in Leviticus 18 and 19. The sequence Leviticus 18 (prohibition) → Leviticus 20 (penalty) is interrupted by Leviticus 19's broad ethical code, which contextualizes the sexual prohibitions within the larger pattern of holiness ethics.
2: Say to the people of Israel: Any one of the people of Israel or of the strangers who sojourn in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death (mot yumat). The people of the land shall stone him with stones — Molech sacrifice (child-burning, cf. Leviticus 18:21; 2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 32:35) is the first and most severe prohibition; the double infinitive absolute mot yumat (dying he shall die) signals irreversible capital punishment. The community execution by stoning makes the entire people the instrument of judgment.
3: I myself will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, to make my sanctuary unclean (lema'an tamme et-miqdashi) and to profane my holy name — the double offense of Molech sacrifice: it defiles the sanctuary (the impurity of child-murder touches even YHWH's holy space) and profanes the Name (Israel's child-sacrifice is a desecration of covenant identity). Even if the community fails to execute the offender (Leviticus 20:4), divine karet applies.
4: And if the people of the land do at all close their eyes to that man when he gives one of his children to Molech, and do not put him to death — communal negligence (looking the other way at Molech sacrifice) creates communal guilt. The following verse (Leviticus 20:5) extends divine judgment to the negligent community, treating silence in the face of atrocity as complicity.
5: Then I will set my face against that man and against his clan and will cut them off from among their people, him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech — the clan (mishpachto) shares in the guilt of the individual Molech worshiper; collective culpability extends to all who follow the same path (ha-zonim acharav, those who whore after Molech). The language of spiritual adultery (znut) frames idolatry as covenant infidelity.
6: If a person turns to mediums (ha-ovot) and necromancers (yidde'onim), whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off — consulting the dead is spiritual adultery (znut) just as Molech worship is; both divert loyalty due to YHWH toward illegitimate supernatural powers. The divine self-initiated karet (Leviticus 20:6) operates even when the community does not formally prosecute.
7: Consecrate yourselves (hitqaddashtem), therefore, and be holy (vihyitem qedoshim), for I am YHWH your God — the positive command embedded within the penalty schedule; holiness is not merely avoiding the prohibited behaviors but actively orienting oneself toward YHWH. The reflexive hitqaddashtem (consecrate yourselves) implies ongoing, personal, intentional separation.
8: Keep my statutes (shemartem et-chuqqotai) and do them; I am YHWH who sanctifies you (ani YHWH meqaddishchem) — the declaration that YHWH is the one who sanctifies Israel (meqaddish) is the ground, not the result, of Israel's holiness; Israel is called to embody a holiness that YHWH first effects. The same formula appears in Leviticus 21:8,15,23; Leviticus 22:9,16,32.
9: For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death (mot yumat); he has cursed his father or his mother; his blood is upon him (damo bo) — the capital offense of cursing parents (Exodus 21:17; Proverbs 20:20) is placed immediately after the holiness call, connecting family order to the covenant structure. The phrase damo bo (his blood is on him) absolves the executioners of blood-guilt; the condemned bears the consequence of his own act.
10: If a man commits adultery (nif'al na'aph) with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death — adultery (Exodus 20:14) is capital for both parties; the equal punishment of man and woman is significant in an ancient Near Eastern context where adultery laws often focused exclusively on the woman. Both ha-na'ef (the adulterer) and ha-no'evet (the adulteress) are subject to mot yumat.
11: If a man lies with his father's wife, he has uncovered his father's nakedness (ervat aviv gilla); both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them — intercourse with a father's wife (stepmother, not biological mother) is incest under the Levitical framework. The language ervat aviv gilla (he has uncovered his father's nakedness) implies violation of the father's domain and honor, not only the woman's personhood.
12: If a man lies with his daughter-in-law (kalato), both of them shall surely be put to death; they have committed perversion (tevel asu); their blood is upon them — tevel (confusion, perversion) is the term used for violation of species/status boundaries; daughter-in-law intercourse violates the family structure that marriage creates. Both parties receive capital punishment.
13: If a man lies with a male as with a woman (mishkevey isha), both of them have committed an abomination (to'evah asu); they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them — to'evah (abomination) is the term applied in Leviticus 18:22; the penalty schedule confirms capital punishment for both parties. The two-term sequence tevel (perverted confusion of order) and to'evah (abomination) covers both natural-order violation and divine-disgust violation.
14: If a man takes a woman and her mother also, it is depravity (zimah hi); he and they shall be burned with fire, that there may be no depravity among you — taking a woman and her mother (Leviticus 18:17) is zimah (wickedness, lewdness); burning is the most severe execution mode in Leviticus, used here and for the high priest's daughter who prostitutes herself (Leviticus 21:9). The collective judgment (all three burned) reflects that all three participated in the violation.
15: If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death (mot yumat), and you shall kill the animal — bestiality (Leviticus 18:23; Exodus 22:19; Deuteronomy 27:21) is capital for the man and requires the killing of the animal; the animal's execution prevents ongoing association with the violation and removes an instrument that caused the defilement.
16: If a woman approaches any animal and lies with it, you shall kill the woman and the animal; they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them — the female parallel: a woman who approaches an animal for sexual contact is equally subject to capital punishment. The symmetrical treatment of male and female bestiality is consistent with the Leviticus 20 pattern of equal penalties for both parties.
17: If a man takes his sister, a daughter of his father or a daughter of his mother, and sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness, it is a disgrace (chesed hu); they shall be cut off (venikhrevu) in the sight of the children of their people — sibling incest (Leviticus 18:9) receives karet rather than immediate capital execution; the term chesed here is an archaic use meaning 'disgrace/reproach' rather than the more common covenant-love meaning. Public karet (in the sight of the people) is social as well as divine exclusion.
18: If a man lies with a woman during her menstrual period (niddah) and uncovers her nakedness, he has made naked her fountain (et-mekorah ereh), and she has uncovered the fountain of her blood. Both of them shall be cut off from among their people — the niddah violation (Leviticus 18:19; Leviticus 15:24) carries karet (not immediate capital punishment) for both parties. The Hebrew mekorah (source, fountain) uses bodily imagery for the generative source; ereh is the same root as ervah (nakedness, the term used throughout Leviticus 18 and 20).
19: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister, for that is to make naked one's relative (et-she'ero ereh); they shall bear their iniquity — aunt-nephew relationships (Leviticus 18:12-13) do not carry capital punishment here but the bearing of iniquity (avon yissau), implying a lesser but still significant degree of guilt. She'er (flesh-relation, close kin) is the basis of the prohibition.
20: If a man lies with his uncle's wife, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness (dodato gilla); they shall bear their sin (chet'am yissau); they shall die childless (ariyrim yamuthu) — uncle's wife violation (Leviticus 18:14) results in dying childless (ariri) rather than execution; the punishment is the absence of covenant continuation — no posterity. Childlessness in the Israelite context was understood as a covenantal consequence, not merely biological misfortune.
21: If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity (niddah hi); he has uncovered his brother's nakedness (achiv gilla); they shall be childless — brother's wife violation (Leviticus 18:16; cf. Deuteronomy 25:5-10 where levirate marriage is the exception that proves the rule) results in childlessness. The tension between the Leviticus 20:21 prohibition and the Deut 25 levirate provision is resolved by noting the Deut 25 exception applies only when the brother died without an heir.
22: You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out (vataqe ha-aretz etchem) — the land-vomiting image from Leviticus 18:25-28 recurs; the land itself responds to moral defilement by expelling its inhabitants. Covenant obedience is not merely personal ethics but the condition for continued habitation in the promised land.
23: And you shall not walk in the customs of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and therefore I detested them — Canaanite practices (all the sexual and religious violations listed in Leviticus 18 and 20) are the reason for Canaanite expulsion; Israel is warned not to replicate the pattern that brought divine judgment on the prior inhabitants. The conquest is simultaneously punishment of Canaan and warning to Israel.
24: But I have said to you, 'You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess, a land flowing with milk and honey.' I am YHWH your God, who has separated you from the peoples (hifdal eschem min-ha'ammim) — the divine promise of possession (yerushah) frames the holiness requirement: separation from the nations' practices is the precondition for inheriting the nations' land. YHWH's separation of Israel from the peoples corresponds to Israel's separation of clean from unclean (Leviticus 20:25).
25: You shall therefore separate (hivdaltem) the clean beast from the unclean, and the unclean bird from the clean. You shall not make yourselves detestable by beast or by bird or by anything with which the ground crawls — the kashrut taxonomy of Lev 11 is recalled here as the dietary dimension of the same separateness principle governing sexual ethics; Israel's dietary, sexual, and social separateness form a unified holiness stance toward the surrounding nations.
26: You shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine (lihyot li) — the climactic statement of the Holiness Code's social and sexual legislation: holiness is YHWH's own character, communicated to Israel through the separation-act of election. The purpose of holiness (lihyot li, to be mine) frames all ethical requirements as expressions of covenantal belonging rather than mere legal compliance.
27: A man or a woman who is a medium (ov) or a necromancer (yidde'oni) shall surely be put to death. They shall be stoned with stones; their blood shall be upon them — the capital penalty for mediums and necromancers (cf. Leviticus 20:6 for the client; here the practitioner) closes the chapter. Stoning is the standard communal execution; the blood-guilt formula absolves the community. Saul's violation of this law at En-dor (1 Samuel 28:7-19) represents the same pattern of covenant infidelity that led to his rejection.
